title: Minneapolis · The Fall and the Plateau
subtitle: FBI UCR 1991–2018 + MPD NIBRS 2019–2026 · 87 neighborhoods · real locations

hook.line: Minneapolis crime, from the 1991 peak to 2018
hook.sub: FBI UCR · then five turbulent, flat years — up close

punchline.sub: −52% under the same FBI measure · Group A has run flat since 2019

copy.chapter2Kicker: CHAPTER 2 · THE LAST 5 YEARS · MPD NIBRS / CC0
copy.chapter2Caption: Monthly reports, 2021–2026. Every dot is a real reported incident location (block-level, sampled).
copy.transitionDesc: The chart counted per year. From here the map counts per month across 87 neighborhoods — every dot a real reported location, anonymized to the block.
copy.methodRecentSub: the last five years · month by month · real locations
copy.methodDotsSub: Every dot is a real reported offense location (block-level). 99.0% map to one of 87 neighborhoods — the rest counted, disclosed, never invented.
copy.methodFootnote: FBI UCR (through 2018) and MPD NIBRS (2019+) are different measures — shown as two chapters, not one line.
copy.quizQuestion: Which Minneapolis neighborhood is the safest?
copy.seamExplain: The ruler changes here — not the city. Through 2018 the gray bars are the FBI's yearly tally of Minneapolis's classic “index” crimes — murder, robbery, assault, burglary, theft. From 2019 the figures switch to MPD's NIBRS “Group A” offenses — an incident-based system covering a broader list of crimes — so the level steps up because the measure widened, not because crime jumped. Compare the shape within one color, never across the dashed line.
copy.sourceLine: Data: Minneapolis Police Department via Open Data Minneapolis (CC0) · block-level locations
copy.coverageText: 99.0% of records mapped to a neighborhood
copy.creditsSources: Sources: MPD NIBRS incident data (2019–present, CC0) via Open Data Minneapolis & FBI UCR (1991–2018) via the FBI Crime Data Explorer (1990 unreported by MPD; earlier years severed by that gap). Neighborhoods: City of Minneapolis. Non-NIBRS context feeds (e.g. Shots Fired Calls, published only from July 2020) are shown as context, never counted as crime. Basemap: © OpenStreetMap contributors (ODbL).

historyNotes.text: The 2000s grind the totals steadily down.
2018: 20,221 — down 52% from the peak; the NIBRS record begins.
2020: property crime surges after late May — the record shows the spike month by month.

annotations.text: Every dot is a real reported offense location — anonymized to the block by MPD (CC0 open data).
July 2022 — the busiest Group A month of the last five years: 4,518.
Downtown West — Minneapolis's busiest neighborhood for Group A crime.
Northeast Park: down 42% since 2022 — the city's biggest drop.
Group A has run flat citywide: 41,473 (2019) → 42,765 (2025).
